nativeRange.habitat: Rocky intertidal and shallow subtidal zones; typically attached to hard substrate in areas with moderate flow
nativeRange.biotope: Temperate to subtropical rocky reef and intertidal

decorPreferences: zip-tie or fishing line to rockwork — do not bury in sand

avoidWith: aggressive herbivorous fish that may graze it heavily

imageCandidates.notes: Kirstie S (CC0), 1536x2048. Complete branching red thallus, vibrant orange-red, sharp and well-lit; single dominant subject showing the fleshy cylindrical branching form. Plant ID confidence inherently low for macroalgae.
Al Kordesch (CC0), 2048x1536. Whole bushy clump held in palm in a tide pool; sharp, good red coloration, clearly shows branching habit. Minor green alga at right edge.

Al Kordesch (CC0), 1536x2048. Close feature shot of the smooth fleshy cylindrical branches, best illustrating the succulent/firmer-than-Gracilaria texture; tight crop so used as non-recommended detail.

summary: Succulent Seaweed (Sarcodiotheca gaudichaudii) is a fleshy, branching red macroalga from the Pacific coast, larger and firmer than most Gracilaria. It is suited to display tanks and must be attached to rockwork rather than buried in sand.
careNotes:
Sarcodiotheca gaudichaudii (family Solieriaceae, order Gigartinales) is a rhodophyte (red alga) native to the Pacific coast of North America and South America, with documented occurrences from Alaska and British Columbia south through California, Mexico, and extending to Peru. Its North Pacific origin suggests some tolerance for cooler water conditions relative to typical tropical reef macroalgae — keepers in cooler systems (down to approximately 20°C) have reported it can be maintained successfully, though the standard reef parameter range of 22–26°C (72–78°F) is generally advised for typical reef tank conditions.

The thallus is described as larger, firmer, and more succulent than similar-looking Gracilaria varieties, growing in a branching pattern. It is sold by Mosaic Macros in 2–3" and 5"+ cutting sizes.

IMPORTANT ATTACHMENT NOTE: As a red alga (Rhodophyta), Sarcodiotheca gaudichaudii will melt at the base if buried in sand. It must be attached to hard substrate — wedging into rockwork crevices is the least stressful method; zip-tie or fishing line attachment is also effective; short-term superglue bonding to rock is acceptable. Do not attempt to anchor via a sand bed.

Lighting: Full-spectrum LED is preferred. Actinic-only spectrum is not recommended and cannot adequately support growth of this or other demanding red macroalgae. Moderate light intensity is generally advised for this species.

Flow: Gentle to moderate, equivalent to a soft-coral reef system. Excessive current may tear the thallus.

Nutrients: Weekly dosing of iron, iodine, and other trace elements is generally advised for macroalgae kept in reef systems. Target nitrate 5–20 ppm and phosphate 0.02–0.1 ppm; calcium 400–450 ppm and magnesium 1,250–1,350 ppm support overall reef health.

Nutrient uptake is considered low for this ornamental red macro — it is not a refugium workhorse species but rather a display specimen. Compatible with typical reef invertebrates (snails, hermit crabs, shrimp). May harbor hitchhiker organisms in wild-collected stock.

Propagation in captivity is by fragmentation: detaching a portion of the thallus and reattaching it to rockwork using the methods above.
breedingNotes: In captivity, propagation is by simple thallus fragmentation, with detached segments reattached to rockwork via wedging, zip-tie or fishing line, or superglue. Sexual reproduction — the gametophyte-sporophyte alternation of generations typical of red algae — occurs in the wild but has not been documented as a meaningful propagation path in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Only known commercial source for this species. Confirms scientific name, branching succulent red thallus form, compatibility with snails/hermit crabs/shrimp/fish, and available cutting sizes (2–3" and 5"+).

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Macroalgae Care Guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source providing authoritative parameter targets (temp 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1,250–1,350 ppm, NO3 5–20 ppm, PO4 0.02–0.1 ppm). Confirms red algae will melt at the base if buried in sand — must attach to rock. Confirms attachment methods: wedging (preferred), zip-tie/fishing line, superglue (short-term). Recommends full-spectrum LED and weekly iron/iodine/trace-element dosing.
